content.text: The Azure Landing Zone Portal Accelerator (formerly the Enterprise-Scale reference implementation) is a web-based wizard in the Azure portal that deploys a complete ESLZ in a single deployment. It generates an ARM template based on your answers and submits it as a tenant-scoped deployment — requiring Global Administrator or Owner on the tenant root management group. No code is needed during the initial deployment. The portal accelerator is the right starting point for organizations that want to evaluate ESLZ design before committing to an IaC toolchain, or for small platform teams that need to be operational in hours rather than weeks.

attrs.alt: Portal Accelerator wizard flow and deployed resources

content.text: The portal accelerator and ALZ-Bicep deploy the same policy baseline. The key difference is lifecycle management: the portal accelerator is a one-time wizard; ALZ-Bicep is a versioned codebase with monthly upstream updates.

The portal accelerator requires three platform subscriptions created before the wizard runs: a management subscription (Log Analytics and Automation), a connectivity subscription (hub networking), and an identity subscription (AADDS or IaaS DCs). Permissions required: Owner or User Access Administrator on the tenant root management group (/), and Owner on each of the three platform subscriptions. Without tenant-root Owner, the management group and policy deployments at tenant scope will fail with authorization errors.
Management group hierarchy and tenant-scoped policy definitions require tenant-level ARM deployments
Log Analytics workspace, Automation Account, Defender plans are deployed into this subscription
Hub VNet, Firewall, Private DNS zones, VPN/ER gateway are deployed into this subscription
AADDS or DC VMs are deployed here; NSGs and VNet linking to hub are subscription-scoped
Required only if you choose Entra Domain Services — AADDS needs Entra tenant admin consent
Redeploying from the Exported ARM Template

After the wizard completes, download the generated ARM template from the Azure portal under Resource Groups → your management subscription → Deployments → alz-<timestamp> → Template → Download. This JSON file is the authoritative record of what was deployed. Store it in git alongside a parameters file so the deployment is reproducible. The same template can be resubmitted with az deployment tenant create to redeploy after manual changes drift the environment.

# Deploy Azure Landing Zone via Portal Accelerator — CLI equivalent
# The portal wizard generates and submits the following ARM deployment types.
# Use these commands to redeploy from the exported ARM template.

# 1. Download the exported ARM template after the wizard completes
#    Portal: Resource Group -> Deployments -> alz-deployment -> Template -> Download

# 2. Review and parameterize the downloaded template
ls azuredeploy.json azuredeploy.parameters.json

# 3. Redeploy from CLI (tenant-scoped, required for Management Group resources)
az deployment tenant create \
  --name 'alz-portal-redeploy-v2' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file azuredeploy.json \
  --parameters @azuredeploy.parameters.json

# 4. What-if before applying changes to an existing ESLZ
az deployment tenant what-if \
  --name 'alz-portal-whatif' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file azuredeploy.json \
  --parameters @azuredeploy.parameters.json \
  --result-format ResourceIdOnly

# 5. Validate the template without deploying
az deployment tenant validate \
  --name 'alz-portal-validate' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file azuredeploy.json \
  --parameters @azuredeploy.parameters.json

# 6. Verify management group hierarchy was created correctly
az account management-group list \
  --query '[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName, Parent:details.parent.displayName}' \
  -o table

# 7. Verify policy definitions are deployed at the alz MG
az policy definition list \
  --management-group alz \
  --query '[?policyType==`Custom`]|length(@)'

The wizard exports a single azuredeploy.json with inline parameters. Split the parameters into a separate azuredeploy.parameters.json file and commit both to source control. The parameters file captures every choice made in the wizard (MG prefix, subscription IDs, networking topology, CIDR ranges, enabled services). Storing these in git creates an audit trail of when configuration choices changed and allows CI pipelines to redeploy via az deployment tenant create on parameter file changes.

The portal accelerator deploys once and does not reconcile future drift. Any manual change to a management group, policy assignment, or networking resource after the wizard completes is invisible to the portal accelerator. Use az deployment tenant what-if with the exported template to produce a drift report. What-if output categorizes resources as Unchanged, Create, Modify, or Delete — any non-Unchanged entry is drift. Address critical policy assignment drift immediately (Deny-effect assignments that were manually removed leave workloads unprotected).

# Detect drift between the portal-accelerator-deployed state and current Azure state
# The portal accelerator does NOT reconcile drift — use what-if as a manual drift check.

# Run what-if against current state — any 'Modify' or 'Delete' entries are drifted resources
az deployment tenant what-if \
  --name 'alz-drift-check' \
  --location eastus \
  --template-file azuredeploy.json \
  --parameters @azuredeploy.parameters.json \
  --result-format FullResourcePayloads 2>&1 | \
  grep -E '^(  [-~+!] |Resource:)' | head -60

# Resource Graph: check whether the MG hierarchy matches what the wizard deployed
az graph query -q "
  ResourceContainers
  | where type =~ 'microsoft.management/managementgroups'
  | project name, displayName, tenantId
  | order by name asc
" --query 'data[]' -o table

# Resource Graph: policy assignments at ALZ root — detect any that were manually removed
az graph query -q "
  PolicyResources
  | where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments'
  | where id startswith '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz/'
  | project name, displayName=properties.displayName, scope=properties.scope
  | order by name asc
" --query 'data[]' -o table

# Check for policy non-compliance across all subscriptions in the ALZ hierarchy
az policy state summarize \
  --management-group alz \
  --query 'results.nonCompliantPolicies' -o json | head -40
Migrating to ALZ-Bicep After Portal Deployment

The portal accelerator is intentionally a starting point. Microsoft's recommended path is to migrate to ALZ-Bicep for ongoing lifecycle management once the initial deployment is stable. ALZ-Bicep is idempotent — running Module 1 (management groups) against an existing portal-accelerator hierarchy produces no changes if the MG prefix matches. The migration proceeds module by module with what-if gates at each step. Pay attention to policy definition differences: ALZ-Bicep tracks the upstream policy baseline monthly, while the portal accelerator bakes in the policy baseline at wizard run time.

# Migrate from Portal Accelerator (ARM) to ALZ-Bicep for ongoing lifecycle management.
# This migration preserves existing resources — ALZ-Bicep is idempotent and will
# reconcile differences between its modules and what the portal accelerator deployed.

# Step 1: Clone ALZ-Bicep and configure your parameter files to match portal accelerator choices
git clone https://github.com/Azure/ALZ-Bicep.git alz-bicep
cd alz-bicep

# Step 2: Set your MG prefix to match what was deployed by the portal accelerator
# Edit infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/parameters/managementGroupsAll.parameters.all.json
# Set: enterpriseScaleCompanyPrefix = 'alz'  (must match portal accelerator choice)

# Step 3: Run what-if for Module 1 — should show no changes if MG hierarchy already exists
az deployment tenant what-if \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/managementGroupsAll.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/managementGroups/parameters/managementGroupsAll.parameters.all.json \
  --location eastus --result-format ResourceIdOnly

# Step 4: Run what-if for Module 2 — policy definitions
# This will show 'Create' entries for any ALZ-Bicep policy definitions not in the portal export
# (ALZ-Bicep and the portal accelerator track slightly different policy baselines)
az deployment mg what-if \
  --template-file infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/alzPolicyDefinitions.bicep \
  --parameters @infra-as-code/bicep/modules/policy/definitions/parameters/alzPolicyDefinitions.parameters.all.json \
  --management-group-id alz --location eastus --result-format ResourceIdOnly

# Step 5: Deploy all 4 modules sequentially once what-if is acceptable
# See the ALZ-Bicep topic for full deployment commands
echo 'Review what-if output before deploying. Pay attention to Modify/Delete entries on policy assignments.'

Five Resource Graph queries verify that the portal accelerator deployed all expected components: the five standard management groups, policy assignment count at alz root (expect 80+ assignments), Log Analytics workspace in the management subscription, Azure Firewall in the connectivity subscription, and private DNS zones for private endpoint resolution. Run these queries immediately after the wizard completes and store the output as a deployment baseline.

// Resource Graph: audit portal accelerator deployment completeness

// 1. Verify all 5 expected top-level management groups exist
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.management/managementgroups'
| where name in ('alz', 'alz-platform', 'alz-landing-zones', 'alz-sandbox', 'alz-decommissioned')
| project name, displayName=properties.displayName
| order by name asc

// 2. Policy assignment count at each MG scope (portal accelerator deploys ~100+ at alz root)
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/policyassignments'
| extend scope = tostring(properties.scope)
| where scope startswith '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz'
| summarize Assignments = count() by scope
| order by Assignments desc

// 3. Check for Log Analytics workspace in management subscription
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.operationalinsights/workspaces'
| where subscriptionId =~ '<management-sub-id>'
| project name, resourceGroup, location, retentionDays=properties.retentionInDays

// 4. Verify Azure Firewall is deployed in connectivity subscription
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.network/azurefirewalls'
| where subscriptionId =~ '<connectivity-sub-id>'
| extend tier = tostring(properties.sku.tier)
| project name, resourceGroup, location, tier

// 5. Private DNS zones deployed in connectivity subscription
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.network/privatednszones'
| where subscriptionId =~ '<connectivity-sub-id>'
| summarize Count = count() by location
Portal Accelerator vs ALZ-Bicep vs ALZ Terraform
None — ARM template is idempotent but no state file
Terraform state file in Azure Blob Storage
Manual re-run of wizard for updates
Monthly upstream merge from ALZ-Bicep repo
Monthly upstream merge from caf-enterprise-scale repo
Manual: az deployment what-if against exported template
Manual: az deployment what-if for each module
Teams already using Bicep / Azure-native stack
Teams already using Terraform / multi-cloud IaC
Running the wizard without placing platform subscriptions first
The connectivity, management, and identity wizard steps fail — the subscriptions do not exist to target
Create the three platform subscriptions and place them in the Tenant Root MG before starting the wizard
Not downloading the ARM template after wizard completion
The only record of the wizard choices is inside Azure; if the subscription is deleted or the deployment expires, the choices are lost
Download azuredeploy.json and azuredeploy.parameters.json immediately after the wizard and commit to git
Re-running the wizard to make a small change
A new wizard run may overwrite manual customizations to policy assignments or networking made since the initial deployment
For post-deployment changes, modify the exported ARM template and redeploy via CLI with what-if; reserve the wizard for net-new ESLZ deployments
Treating the portal accelerator as a long-term management tool
Policy definitions in the exported ARM template become stale as Microsoft releases new built-in policies; no upstream sync path exists in the portal accelerator
Migrate to ALZ-Bicep or Terraform within 6 months; use the portal accelerator only as a starting point
Not running what-if before re-deploying the exported template
Deny-effect policy assignments deployed by the wizard can block existing resources if the parameter file was modified to add new assignments
Always run az deployment tenant what-if before az deployment tenant create on an existing ESLZ
Choosing vWAN topology in the wizard when hub-spoke is the team standard
vWAN and hub-spoke networking are fundamentally different architectures; switching after deployment requires destroying and recreating all spoke peerings and route tables
Align the networking topology choice in the wizard with the long-term network design before running the wizard

The Portal Accelerator deploys a complete ESLZ hierarchy via a zero-code wizard in the Azure portal. It requires Owner at tenant root and three pre-existing platform subscriptions. After deployment, download the exported ARM template and commit it to source control as the authoritative baseline. Use az deployment tenant what-if with the exported template for drift detection. For long-term lifecycle management, migrate to ALZ-Bicep or ALZ Terraform — the portal accelerator is a starting point, not an ongoing management tool.
